question: A 47-year-old woman has progressive cognitive and behavioral deficits with motor impairment over approximately 3 years. Her father died of progressive dementia. The brain MRI shown is obtained. Based on the imaging and family history, what is the most likely probability that a biological child of this patient will inherit the causative pathogenic variant?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the MRI image. The midline sagittal brain scan shows marked thinning of the corpus callosum and generalized central white-matter volume loss, findings that are compatible with a progressive neurodegenerative leukoencephalopathy in a relatively young adult.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical stem, which describes a 47-year-old woman with progressive cognitive/behavioral decline and motor impairment over ~3 years and a father who died of progressive dementia. The presence of affected individuals in two successive generations (father then daughter) indicates vertical transmission of disease in the family.

Then, I consider how different inheritance patterns would fit that family pattern and the question about the chance a biological child will inherit the causative pathogenic variant. A dominant trait passed from an affected parent to an affected child is most consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance, in which a single pathogenic allele in the parent can be transmitted to offspring. Under autosomal dominant inheritance, each child of an affected parent has a 50% (one-half) probability of inheriting the pathogenic variant, independent of sex.

Finally, I map that expected probability onto the answer choices. The option consistent with autosomal dominant vertical transmission and a 1-in-2 risk to each child is 50%.

Therefore, based on the imaging-supported familial neurodegenerative pattern and the pedigree showing affected parent and affected child, the most likely probability for each biological child to inherit the pathogenic variant is 50%.
caption: Brain MRI features of CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy. The images were obtained from a 47-year-old female with progressive cognitive and behavioral deficits, and motor impairments with an approximate 3-year disease duration. Her father died of progressive dementia. The commercial genetic testing demonstrated the presence of heterozygote CSF1R mutation, p.L786S, c.2357 T > C. a, b Restricted diffusion in the splenium and left body of corpus callosum. c Thinning of corpus callosum on the sagittal section. d T2 hyperintensities in frontal and parietal white matter on the axial section. Courtesy of Daniel F. Broderick, M.D., Mayo Clinic Florida
sub_caption: c Thinning of corpus callosum on the sagittal section.
image_context: A diagnostic value of unique calcifications distributed in selective brain regions on computed tomography scans has been proposed [16]. In addition, thinning of the corpus callosum, extensive non-confluent and occasionally even confluent subcortical white matter lesions on T2-weighted brain MRI images, and persistent restricted diffusion of diffuse-weighted imaging signal without gadolinium enhancement can be evident during the disease course. This may help in identifying potential CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy cases (Fig. <xref rid="40478_2020_1093_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>) [) [17]. We have previously demonstrated that the brain volume fractions measured by MRI did not differ significantly between CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy and MS patients, while the cerebellum was relatively spared in CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy patients [18]. We also employed an artificial intelligence machine learning to differentiate this disease state from MS using multi-parametric brain MRI images [19].Fig. 1Brain MRI features of CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy. The images were obtained from a 47-year-old female with progressive cognitive and behavioral deficits, and motor impairments with an approximate 3-year disease duration. Her father died of progressive dementia. The commercial genetic testing demonstrated the presence of heterozygote CSF1R mutation, p.L786S, c.2357 T > C. a, b Restricted diffusion in the splenium and left body of corpus callosum. c Thinning of corpus callosum on the sagittal section. d T2 hyperintensities in frontal and parietal white matter on the axial section. Courtesy of Daniel F. Broderick, M.D., Mayo Clinic Florida
relevant_image_context: A diagnostic value of unique calcifications distributed in selective brain regions on computed tomography scans has been proposed [16]. In addition, thinning of the corpus callosum, extensive non-confluent and occasionally even confluent subcortical white matter lesions on T2-weighted brain MRI images, and persistent restricted diffusion of diffuse-weighted imaging signal without gadolinium enhancement can be evident during the disease course. This may help in identifying potential CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy cases (Fig. 1) [17]. We have previously demonstrated that the brain volume fractions measured by MRI did not differ significantly between CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy and MS patients, while the cerebellum was relatively spared in CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy patients [18]. We also employed an artificial intelligence machine learning to differentiate this disease state from MS using multi-parametric brain MRI images [19].Fig. 1 Brain MRI features of CSF1R-related leukoencephalopathy. The images were obtained from a 47-year-old female with progressive cognitive and behavioral deficits, and motor impairments with an approximate 3-year disease duration. Her father died of progressive dementia. The commercial genetic testing demonstrated the presence of heterozygote CSF1R mutation, p.L786S, c.2357 T > C. c Thinning of corpus callosum on the sagittal section. Courtesy of Daniel F.
